content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
September 5, 1997
Mitchel B. Kahn, Esq.
England, Whitfield, Schroeder & Tredway, LLP
300 Esplanade Drive, Sixth Floor
Oxnard, California 93030
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-97-444
Dear Mr. Kahn:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf ofBob Hamilton, a
committee treasurer, regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
QUESTION
May a treasurer coordinate a golf tournament to raise funds for a city councilmember’s
officeholder account and a charitable community foundation by carefully segregating the source
of the funds and the bank accounts in which the funds will be deposited?
CONCLUSION
The treasurer may coordinate the fundraising event by carefully segregating the source of
the funds and the bank accounts in which the funds will be deposited as long as the charitable
community foundation is not a controlled committee as defined by regulation 18217. To comply
with the officeholder provisions of the Act, the treasurer must receive separate checks for the
nonprofit organization and the officeholder account.
FACTS
Bob Hamilton is the treasurer of a charitable community fund in the name of a city
councilmember and is also the treasurer for the city councilmember’s officeholder account. This
fall, the city councilmember is sponsoring a golf tournament that will raise funds for both the
Government Code sections 81000- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. 1-97-444
Page 2
officeholder account and the charitable community fund. The officeholder account funds will be
raised through the sale of Tee sponsorships only. All other sponsorships, entry fees, silent
auction and thawing prizes will benefit the charitable community fund, which is administered by
a nonprofit, 501(c)(3) community foundation. All expenses for the tournament will be paid from
entry fees or will be covered directly by donated goods or services. The fact that the Tee
sponsorships are being placed in an officeholder account and other contributions are for a
charitable purpose is clearly indicated on all advertisements, handbills and entry forms.
Mr. Hamilton will segregate the source of the funds and the bank accounts in which the funds
will be deposited.
ANALYSIS
Under section 85313, an elected officer may establish an officeholder account for
expenses related to assisting, serving or communicating with constituents or to carrying out the
official duties ofthe elected officer. Contributions to the officeholder account may not exceed
$250 per person per year. (Section 853 13(b).) In addition, officeholder account funds may not
be used in connection with any campaign for elective office. (Section 85313(a).)
Nonprofit Organization as Candidate’s Controlled Committee
You would like to know whether Mr. Hamilton may coordinate a joint fundraiser to raise
funds for a city councilmember’s officeholder account and a charitable community foundation.
Section 85313 permits an elected officer to raise funds for the purpose of establishing an
officeholder account. The Act does not prohibit an officeholder from co-hosting a joint
fundraising event with a nonprofit organization for the purpose of establishing an officeholder
account. (Marenco Advice Letter, No. A-96-373, Goodin Advice Letter, No. A-94-290.)
Please note, however, the contribution limitations contained in section 85301 set limits on
contributions made “to any candidate or the candidate’s controlled committee.” (Sections 82016,
85301 )2 Section 85305 contains time restrictions on when a candidate may accept contributions.
Contributions a candidate solicits for himself or herself, or for the candidate’s controlled
committee, will be subject to the limits set forth in sections 85301 and 85305. Conversely, if the
candidate solicits contributions for a committee or organization not controlled by the candidate,
then the limits in sections 85301 and 85305 do not apply.
If a candidate solicits funds for a nonprofit association, the limitations in sections 85301
and 85305 will not apply unless the association is the candidate’s controlled committee.
2 The contribution limitations and restrictions set forth in sections 85301-85312 do not apply to an elected
officer’s controlled committee fonned for the purpose of accepting contributions to an officeholder account.
(Regulation 18531.4.)


File No. 1-97-444
Page 3
Regulation 18217 (copy enclosed) provides a definition of controlled committee applicable to
organizations that are tax-exempt under section 501 of the Internal Revenue Code, provided they
are not formed or existing primarily for political purposes. (See Regulation 18217(f).)
Regulation 18217(a) provides that a nonprofit association will be considered a controlled
committee if:
“(1) A candidate, his or her agent, or any committee he or
she controls, exercises significant influence over the actions and
decisions of the organization, or acts jointly with the organization
in connection with the making of expenditures.
(2) The organization qualifies as a committee under
Government Code Section 820 13(a), and the organization is
operated for political purposes. For purposes of this regulation, an
organization is ‘operated for political purposes’ if either of the
following applies:
(A) The organization receives or expends funds for the
purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the
voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or
the qualification or passage of any measure.
(B) The organization makes contributions to candidates or
their controlled committees.”
A candidate may be involved with a nonprofit organization without the entity becoming
an additional controlled committee of the candidate if certain criteria are met. Regulation
18217(b) provides that a nonprofit organization is presumed not to be significantly influenced by
a candidate if:
“(1) The candidate is not substantially involved in the
day-to-day operations ofthe organization, and the organization is
controlled by a board of directors with 3 or more members,
two-thirds ofwhom are not:
(A) Candidates;
(B) Agents, campaign staff, employees, or persons
otherwise under the control of a candidate; or
(C) Brothers, sisters, parents, children, spouses,
brothers-in-law, sisters-in-law, sons-in-law,
daughters-in-law, mothers-in-law or fathers-in-law
of a board member who is a candidate.
(2) The name of the organization does not include the
name of the candidate. For purposes ofthis subdivision (b)(2) the
term ‘name of the candidate’ means the candidate’s first and last
name or some other unambiguous reference to the candidate.”


File No. 1-97-444
Page 4
Accordingly, Mr. Hamilton may only coordinate the joint fundraiser with the nonprofit
organization without violating the contribution limits and restrictions ofProposition 208 if the
charitable community foundation is not a controlled committee as defined by regulation 18217.
(See Bovée Advice Letter, No. 1-97-027, Leidigh Advice Letter, No. A-97-140, Kopp Advice
Letter, No. A-97-108, copies enclosed.) You must examine regulation 18217 to determine
whether Mr. Hamilton’s status as treasurer ofboth the charitable community fund and the
officeholder account poses a problem under the Act.
Segregating Contributions and Proceeds
You would also like to know whether Mr. Hamilton will comply with the Act by
segregating the source ofthe donations and the bank accounts in which the proceeds will be
deposited. Aggregate contributions to an officeholder account must not exceed $10,000 per year.
(Section 853 13(a).) Once an officeholder raises $10,000 into an officeholder account, the
officeholder may not raise additional funds into the account until the next calendar year. (Weldy
Advice Letter, No. A-96-33 1.) Thus, an officeholder may not deposit into the officeholder
account any proceeds from a joint fundraising event that exceed $10,000, including proceeds
earmarked for the nonprofit organization. In addition, we have advised that when a candidate
holds a joint fundraiser with a nonprofit organization, the candidate and the nonprofit
organization must receive separate checks. (Brown Advice Letter, No. A-97-367.) For example,
if a ticket costs $10 and the candidate and the nonprofit organization are splitting the proceeds
evenly, the contributor must write two checks for $5, one to the candidate and one to the
nonprofit organization.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Julia Butcher
Graduate Legal Assistant, Legal Division
SGC:IB:jlw
Enclosures

nonprofit organization and the officeholder account.

QUESTION: May a treasurer coordinate a golf tournament to raise funds for a city councilmember’s
officeholder account and a charitable community foundation by carefully segregating the source
of the funds and the bank accounts in which the funds will be deposited?

the funds and the bank accounts in which the funds will be deposited as long as the charitable
community foundation is not a controlled committee as defined by regulation 18217. To comply
with the officeholder provisions of the Act, the treasurer must receive separate checks for the
nonprofit organization and the officeholder account.
